title: Scottish shortbread
subtitle: Three-ingredient butter shortbread, baked pale and snapped thick.
excerpt: Butter, flour and icing sugar combined into a dough that bakes to a pale, crumbling, deeply buttery biscuit. The Scottish original uses rice flour for its sandy texture. Makes about twenty fingers or twelve rounds.
sourceNotes: Scottish shortbread. Shortbread was associated with Scotland from at least the 16th century, where it was made from the twice-baked bread that was then shortened with butter. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861, public domain) contains a shortbread recipe. The traditional Scottish version uses rice flour alongside plain flour for a sandier, more crumbling texture. It is particularly associated with Hogmanay (New Year) and Christmas.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked shortbread for up to 3 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for 2 weeks in an airtight tin.
recipe.temperatureNote: Bake at a low temperature; shortbread must stay pale.

content.text: Shortbread is baked at a low temperature and must stay pale gold, never brown. Browning hardens the biscuit and changes the flavour from buttery and delicate to nutty and dry. A low oven and a short baking time is the correct approach. Use the best butter you can buy; it is most of the flavour.

items.prepNote: for the sandy texture; plain flour works but the texture is different

content.text: Make the dough, chill and cut

Beat {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and {{icing-sugar}} of icing sugar together until pale and creamy. Add {{plain-flour}} of plain flour, {{rice-flour}} of rice flour and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and mix until a dough forms. Press together into a flat disc, wrap and rest for 30 minutes. Roll out to 1 cm thick on a lightly floured surface. Cut into fingers or rounds and place on a lined baking tray.

Bake at 160°C for 18 to 22 minutes until firm and very lightly golden at the edges. They should not colour. While still warm, dust with a little extra caster or icing sugar if liked. Cool completely on the tray before lifting; shortbread is fragile when warm.

items.cause: Over-baked or browning at the edges.
items.fix: Check at 18 minutes. The biscuit should be pale gold at most. If it is browning, reduce the oven temperature by 10°C next time.

items.symptom: Crumbles when lifted from the tray
items.fix: Leave on the tray until completely cool. Shortbread sets as it cools.

items.cause: Dough overworked after the flour was added.
items.fix: Mix the flour in briefly, just until a dough forms. Overworking develops gluten and produces a tougher biscuit.

content.text: Scottish shortbread is one of the most recognisable British biscuits internationally, carried around the world in tartan tins and sold as a souvenir. At home in Scotland, it is made for Hogmanay and Christmas and given as a gift. Three ingredients, the best butter, a cool oven. The result does not need improvement.
